name: Mental Health
description: Disorders, treatments, milestones, and the people who shaped how we think about the mind.

questions.q: Which month is observed as Mental Health Awareness Month in the United States?

source.original_a: Raising awareness of mental health issues

questions.q: On what date is World Mental Health Day observed each year?

source.notes: Established 1992 by the World Federation for Mental Health.

questions.q: Which 19th-century reformer crusaded for humane treatment of the mentally ill in U.S. asylums?

source.notes: Lobbied successfully for the founding of state psychiatric hospitals in the 1840s-50s.

questions.q: Whose book 'A Mind That Found Itself' (1908) catalyzed the U.S. mental hygiene movement?

source.notes: Beers also founded the National Committee for Mental Hygiene (now Mental Health America) in 1908.

questions.q: Which Austrian neurologist founded psychoanalysis?
Which Swiss psychiatrist developed analytical psychology and concepts like the collective unconscious and archetypes?

source.notes: Once Freud's protégé before the famous split in 1913.

questions.q: What does 'DSM' stand for, the standard U.S. classification of mental disorders?
choices: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders

source.notes: Published by the American Psychiatric Association. Currently in DSM-5-TR.

questions.q: Which mental health organization runs the U.S. Suicide & Crisis Lifeline at 988?
choices: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)

source.notes: 988 launched as the new three-digit shortcut in July 2022.

questions.q: What does 'PTSD' stand for?

source.notes: Formally added to the DSM in 1980.

questions.q: Which mood disorder is characterized by alternating periods of elevated mood (mania) and depression?
Which anxiety disorder is characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors performed to relieve them?
Which therapy approach focuses on changing unhelpful thought patterns and behaviors?

source.notes: Developed by Aaron Beck in the 1960s; widely considered the gold-standard talk therapy for anxiety and depression.

questions.q: Approximately how many U.S. adults experience some form of mental illness in any given year?
Which class of antidepressants — including Prozac and Zoloft — became dominant in the 1990s?
Which mood-stabilizing medication is most commonly used to treat bipolar disorder?

source.notes: Discovered as a psychiatric treatment by John Cade in 1949.

questions.q: What is the most common form of color-blindness — and one of the more common neurological-perception conditions?

source.notes: Affects roughly 8% of men and 0.5% of women.

questions.q: Which 1973 American Psychiatric Association decision removed homosexuality from the DSM?

source.notes: Removed in 1973 in a revision to DSM-II following pressure from the gay rights movement.

questions.q: What therapy uses ECT — applying electrical currents to the brain — and is still used for severe, treatment-resistant depression?

source.notes: Modern ECT is far gentler than the dramatized depictions in 'One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest.'

questions.q: What is the name of the U.S. mental-health awareness foundation co-founded in 1979 by parents of mentally ill people?

source.notes: NAMI is the largest grassroots mental-health organization in the U.S.

questions.q: Which Austrian psychiatrist developed logotherapy after surviving the Holocaust, and wrote 'Man's Search for Meaning'?

source.notes: audit-corrected 2026-05-09: Frankl was Austrian (born Vienna 1905), not Czech-Austrian. He survived Theresienstadt and Auschwitz. Book has sold over 16 million copies worldwide.
